text:
Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders (2021) 51:1015–1027 
https://doi.org/10.1007/s10803-020-04582-5
ORIGINAL PAPER

Adaptation of One‑Session Treatment for Specific Phobias for Children 
with Autism Spectrum Disorder Using a Non‑concurrent Multiple 
Baseline Design: A Preliminary Investigation
Thomas Ollendick1 · Ashley Muskett1,2 · Sarah R. Radtke1 · Isaac Smith1

Published online: 1 July 2020 
© Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2020

Abstract
Anxiety disorders are among the most common co-occurring disorders for individuals with ASD. Several adaptations to 
cognitive behavioral approaches have been proposed for this population (Moree & Davis, 2010). The current study examined 
feasibility and preliminary efficacy of an ASD-specific adaptation of one-session treatment (OST) for specific phobia (SP). 
Standard OST consists of one 3-h session followed by four weekly phone calls. Modifications for ASD included increased 
parental involvement, use of visual aids, and inclusion of four 1-h booster sessions in place of the four weekly phone calls. 
Visual inspection and Friedman tests revealed significant reductions in fear ratings and phobia severity from pre- to post-
treatment and follow-up assessments. Modest changes were observed in behavioral avoidance. These findings provide initial 
evidence that this treatment merits further study.
Keywords  Autism spectrum disorder · Specific phobia · One-session treatment
Introduction

Autism spectrum disorder (ASD), a neurodevelopmental 
disorder characterized by the presence of social difficulties 
as well as the presence of restricted and repetitive behaviors 
(APA 2013), affects as many as 1 in 58 children (Maen-
ner et al. 2020). Recent work has suggested that in addi-
tion to these core features, youth with these disorders are 
at increased risk for various other forms of psychopathol-
ogy including anxiety and its disorders (Kent and Simonoff 
2017; Simonoff et al. 2008). Over the past 10–15 years, 
much work has examined the co-occurrence of anxiety with 
ASD (Magiati et al. 2017; Kent and Simonoff 2017).
Prevalence and Presentation of Anxiety in ASD

There is broad consensus that anxiety disorders are among 
the most common comorbidities of ASD. Although preva-
lence estimates vary, a recent book chapter reports figures 
of approximately 50% (Kent and Simonoff 2017). In many 
cases, anxiety disorders in youth with ASD present with 
symptoms similar to those seen in typically developing 
youth. However, some studies suggest specific considera-
tions for children with ASD may be necessary. For example, 
children with ASD may experience more “atypical” anxi-
ety symptoms (Kerns and Kendall 2012), such as general 
anxiety surrounding disruption in daily routine or specific 
phobias of a baby crying or the texture of a dog’s fur. Fur-
thermore, symptoms of anxiety disorders may be difficult to 
identify in this population due to overlap with the symptoms 
of ASD (White et al. 2009a, b). Additionally, many families 
may not seek treatment for anxiety disorders in their child 
with ASD due to the belief that core deficits associated with 
ASD may take precedence over the treatment of anxiety in 
these youth (Mason and Scior 2004).

1016
	
Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders (2021) 51:1015–1027
Specific Phobia (SP) in ASD

SP is defined as excessive fear and avoidance related to 
a specific object or situation. In the DSM-5 (APA 2013), 
SPs are categorized into five major subtypes: animal type, 
natural environment type, blood-injection-injury type, sit-
uational type, and other type (e.g., choking, loud noises, 
costumed characters). Current reviews suggest that clini-
cally significant phobias may be present in up to 20% of 
typically developing youth and up to 30% of youth with 
ASD, rendering SP the most common psychiatric comor-
bidity associated with ASD (Oar et al. 2019; Van Steensel 
et al. 2011). For many children, phobias result in consid-
erable academic, social, and personal distress, as well as 
interference in day-to-day functioning (Essau et al. 2000; 
Ollendick et al. 2004). Moreover, they may lead to other 
anxiety, mood, and substance use disorders (Kendall et al. 
2004; Roberts et al. 2016). However, little is currently 
known about the presentation of SP in ASD.
It has been suggested that increased rates of sensory 
processing difficulties, particularly sensory sensitivity, 
may be related to the increased prevalence of SP in ASD 
via classical conditioning processes (Amos et al. 2019; 
Green and Ben-Sasson 2010; Muskett et al. 2019). For 
example, if a child is particularly sensitive to loud noises, 
the sound of a balloon popping or a baby crying may be 
more aversive than it would be to a less sensitive child 
and thus be more likely to result in fear and an avoid-
ance response. This is supported by several studies on the 
relationship of sensory processing to anxiety (Ben-Sasson 
et al. 2008; Green et al. 2012). For instance, Ben-Sasson 
et al. (2008) found that toddlers with ASD who demon-
strate high frequency of sensory symptoms (including 
over-responsivity) showed higher rates of anxiety than 
those with low frequency of similar sensory symptoms.
In addition to sensory processing difficulties, other 
core ASD characteristics are likely implicated in the 
development and maintenance of anxiety symptoms as 
well. Restricted and repetitive behaviors manifesting as 
an insistence on sameness have also been proposed as a 
key contributor to anxious symptoms in individuals with 
ASD. Intolerance of uncertainty and insistence on same-
ness have both been associated with increased anxiety 
among individuals with ASD (Magiati et al. 2017). While 
these factors are commonly associated with generalized 
anxiety, they may also bear relevance to the etiology of SP 
in youth with ASD. Where sensory processing difficulties 
may contribute to the development of idiosyncratic SPs 
(Kerns et al. 2015), more common SPs may be caused in 
part by difficulty coping with the uncertainty associated 
with the phobic stimulus. For example, an animal phobia 
may be associated with a belief that the behavior of the

animal cannot be predicted and that there is an exagger-
ated risk of the animal engaging in a harmful behavior; 
similarly, a situational phobia of darkness may be associ-
ated with uncertainty around what cannot be seen. Rigid 
thinking associated with these beliefs in youth with ASD 
may render them much more difficult to change (Ollendick 
and White 2012).
Treating Anxiety in ASD

While research on the underlying mechanism of anxiety dis-
orders and specific phobia in ASD remains ongoing, several 
studies have examined the potential of various treatments for 
anxiety and SP in this population. For example, systematic 
reviews by Lang et al. (2010) and Vasa et al. (2014) sug-
gest that Cognitive Behavior Therapy (CBT) is a generally 
effective treatment for anxiety in ASD. CBT is a form of 
therapy that utilizes cognitive strategies as well as behavio-
ral techniques such as behavioral rehearsal, modeling, and 
positive reinforcement to decrease anxiety driven avoid-
ance behaviors. Cognitive techniques involve challenging 
maladaptive cognitions (e.g., “if a bee comes near me it will 
sting me”) with more adaptive beliefs via opportunities for 
learning (e.g., learning that a bee will likely fly away from 
you if you move towards it; see Lang et al. 2010). One of 
the most common cognitive behavioral therapies for anxi-
ety is exposure therapy, in which children are exposed to 
the feared object or situation in order to decrease avoidance 
and provide opportunities for new learning to occur. Given 
the support for CBT in these reviews, a recent review and 
meta-analysis conducted by Ung et al. (2015) specifically 
examined the efficacy of CBT for children and youth with 
“high-functioning” ASD. This meta-analysis included 14 
studies and over 500 youth with ASD. It was determined 
in this review that CBT showed considerable efficacy for 
children with “high-functioning” ASD.
Overall, CBT interventions for youth with ASD have 
required a range of adaptations to protocols initially devel-
oped for typically developing youth. Many of these adapta-
tions have their origins in the large body of literature on the 
development and refinement of strengths-based behavioral 
approaches for skill building in youth with ASD. Core prin-
ciples of applied behavior analysis (ABA) have been applied 
in dozens of iterations, but typically include such common 
components as structured teaching, use of visual supports, 
and parent involvement (Vismara and Rogers 2010). More 
specifically, in the treatment of specific phobias in youth 
with ASD, Moree and Davis (2010) reported that adapta-
tions to CBT for ASD have commonly included modified, 
disorder-specific fear hierarchies, an increased reliance on 
concrete visual cues, incorporation of specific interests, and 
substantial parent involvement. More recent work has sug-
gested that CBT-based therapies for individuals with ASD

1017
Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders (2021) 51:1015–1027

might include a stronger emphasis on behavioral (rather than 
cognitive) components of treatment, and concurrent consid-
eration of social and adaptive challenges (White et al. 2018).
Given the support for CBT in treating anxiety in children 
with ASD, it is important to consider which CBT treatments 
are available for the treatment of SP specifically and whether 
they might be appropriate for youth with ASD. One such 
possible treatment is what is referred to as One-Session 
Treatment (OST; Davis et al. 2019; Öst 1989; Öst et al. 
2001). OST is a brief and intensive 3-h treatment utilizing 
both behavioral and cognitive techniques to expose children 
to their feared object or situation. OST has been established 
as an evidence-based treatment for SP for typically develop-
ing children (Davis et al. 2019). There is also some prelimi-
nary work on the efficacy of this treatment for youth with 
ASD. For example, a case study conducted by Davis and 
colleagues demonstrated support for OST in a child with 
ASD and SPs of water and heights (Davis et al. 2007). Addi-
tionally, OST is a potentially effective treatment for ASD for 
several reasons including the potential for quicker gains in 
a massed-practice setting such as OST and the potential for 
in-depth parent involvement (see Ollendick and Ost (2001); 
Ollendick et al. 2009, 2015).
The present non-concurrent multiple-baseline design 
study sought to examine the initial feasibility and efficacy 
of a version of OST that was modified for children with 
ASD (for modifications see ‘Procedures’ below). OST was 
selected as the treatment of choice based on its demonstrated 
efficacy with typically developing youth (Ollendick et al. 
2009, 2015; Öst et al. 2001) as well as its foundations in 
CBT, which has been shown to be moderately effective for 
children with ASD, albeit with large individual differences 
in treatment response (Vasa et al. 2014). Additionally, due 
to its brief nature, it was reasoned that this treatment may 
be especially good for families with ASD who are often 
addressing many different treatment concerns (Mason and 
Scior 2004). It was hypothesized that the children in this 
study would demonstrate significantly less specific phobia 
symptomology compared to baseline after completing the 
treatment program.
Method
Participants

Participants included nine children and at least one parent 
per child. Inclusion criteria were as follows. The child (a) 
was between 6 and 14 years of age, (b) had a reliable diagno-
sis of ASD, (c) met DSM-5 criteria for Specific Phobia, and 
(d) demonstrated an IQ greater than 70. Exclusion criteria 
included the presence of severe comorbid psychopathology 
warranting other or more immediate treatment (e.g., lower

IQ, psychosis, or severe depression). These children were 
referred elsewhere for treatment. All children included in the 
study (N = 9) had a diagnosis of ASD, were verbally fluent, 
and had an IQ of 70 or above. Of these nine participants, 
67% were White/Caucasian (n = 6), 22% were Black/Afri-
can-American (n = 2), and 11% were Asian/Pacific Islander 
(n = 1). All children were male. Mean annual family income 
was $77,571 (range = $28,000–$225,000). All children met 
criteria for ASD as well as SP. Of the nine children, two 
were taking medication for additional psychiatric concerns 
including Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder and sei-
zures (n = 1) and anxiety (n = 1). All children, in addition 
to meeting criteria for ASD and at least one SP, also met 
criteria for at least one additional comorbid psychiatric dis-
order. The IQ of the children in the study ranged from 81 
to 121 (M = 94.22; SD = 14.5). Comparison scores on the 
Autism Diagnostic Observation Schedule, Second Edition 
(see below, Lord et al. 2012) ranged from 1 to 10 (M = 6.5, 
SD = 2.98). The mean score of 6.5 suggests a moderate level 
of Autism symptoms compared to other children with ASD.
Measures

See Table 1 for participant characteristics and scores on pre-
treatment measures.
Measures of Baseline Participant Characteristics

Autism Diagnostic Observation Schedule, Second Edition 
(Lord et  al. 2012)  The Autism Diagnostic Observation 
Schedule (ADOS-2) was administered to all participants. 
The ADOS-2 is considered a best-practice measure for 
diagnosis of ASD, and large-scale studies have supported 
its reliability and validity (Gotham et al. 2008). Module 3 of 
the ADOS-2, appropriate for children and adolescents with 
fluent speech, was used for all participants. The ADOS-2 
was administered in each case by advanced graduate clini-
cians trained to research reliability in administration and 
coding. The ADOS-2 yields a comparison score indicating 
how characteristic a particular child’s presentation is of the 
symptoms associated with ASD (range = 1–10).

Short Sensory Profile (SSP; McIntosh et al. 1999)  The Short 
Sensory Profile is a measure of a child’s responses to sen-
sory stimuli in their environment. It is a 38-item, caregiver 
report measure. This questionnaire provides information 
on whether children experience several domains of sensory 
experiences differently than typically developing children. 
Domains assessed include tactile sensitivity, visual/audi-
tory sensitivity, low energy/weakness, and overall sensory 
experience. The SSP yields a total score where lower scores 
indicate more sensory differences, with scores less than 141 
being indicative of a definite difference in sensory process-

1018
	
Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders (2021) 51:1015–1027

ing compared to a typical sample. This measure is broadly 
used to assess sensory symptoms in youth with ASD and 
offers adequate psychometric properties (Lane et al. 2011). 
Given the potential for sensory components of ASD to con-
tribute to the development and maintenance of SP symp-
toms, the SSP was included as a means of further character-
izing our sample.

Wechsler Abbreviated Scale of Intelligence, Second Edition 
(WASI‑II; Wechsler 2011)  Two subscales of the WASI-II 
were administered to all participants to ensure participants 
met inclusion criteria with regard to cognitive functioning. 
Scores on the two subtests (Vocabulary and Matrix Reason-
ing) were used to estimate a Full Scale IQ score (FSIQ). 
The two-subtest version can be administered in 15 min and 
offers strong reliability and validity (Wechsler 2011).
Outcome Measures

Anxiety Disorders Interview Schedule for  DSM‑5, Par‑
ent and  Child Versions (ADIS‑P/C; Silverman and  Albano 
1996)  The ADIS-C/P is a semi-structured diagnostic inter-
view that assesses for the presence and severity of a range 
of disorders. Symptomatology is measured using a clinician 
severity rating (CSR), a 9-point scale in which scores equal 
to or greater than four are indicative of clinical severity for 
a given disorder. Previous explorations of the ADIS-P/C 
within ASD have indicated excellent inter-rater reliability, 
with diagnostic agreement not differing as a function of 
presence or absence of ASD diagnosis (Ung et al. 2015).

In the current study, the complete ADIS-P was adminis-
tered to the primary caregiver of each participant at base-
line, while only the specific phobia module of the ADIS-C 
was administered to each child by a second clinician. At 
post-treatment and all follow-up appointments, the top three 
endorsed diagnoses were re-assessed using the ADIS-P, 
while the specific phobia module of the ADIS-C was re-
administered to all children. All study clinicians were bach-
elor’s-level or master’s level graduate students trained to 
reliability. In order to meet criteria for reliability, clinicians 
were required to co-code previously recorded ADIS adminis-
trations. Criteria specified three consecutive administrations 
with no greater than one point difference on CSRs, with no 
disagreement on clinical versus nonclinical CSRs (i.e., no 
disagreements on CSRs of three or four). This interview 
was administered and coded by a clinician who had been 
trained and coded to a reliability of 80% or higher on three 
consecutive ADIS-P-5s with a reliable clinician. Following 
assessment at each time point, consensus diagnoses were 
assigned on the basis of the independent ADIS-P and ADIS-
C administrations; consensus meetings took place under the 
supervision of a doctoral-level clinical psychologist. Pres-
ence of diagnosis (i.e., CSR ≥ 4) and improvement in CSRs 
over time were used as indicators of treatment effects.

Behavioral Approach Task (BAT; Ollendick et  al. 2009; Öst 
et  al. 2001)  A standardized Behavioral Approach Task 
(BAT) was completed by each participant at pre- and post-
treatment, as well as the 6-month follow-up. BATs involved 
asking each child to approach the object of their phobia 
in vivo (e.g., climb a ladder, sit in a room with no lights on,

Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders (2021) 51:1015–1027

watch a confederate chew a piece of bubblegum); each task 
consisted of a series of increasingly difficult steps. Stand-
ardized steps and scripts were available for common pho-
bias (e.g., dark, insects), and were adapted as necessary for 
atypical SPs (see examples in Table 2). Participants were 
instructed to do their best, but that they could end the task 
at any point. BATs were conducted first with the participant 
alone, and then a second time when accompanied by their 
primary caregiver. This second BAT with the parent is used 
to observe what the parent does to either help or impede 
approach behaviors to the phobic stimulus (Ollendick et al. 
2012). Caregivers were instructed that they should “feel free 
to help [participant] as much as they would like.” In most 
cases, this took the form of verbal encouragement by car-
egivers or even modeling behaviors to approach the stimu-
lus. Number of steps for each BAT varied by phobia, and as 
such percentage of steps completed was used as a measure 
of BAT completion. Subjective Units of Distress (SUDs) 
were also reported before and after completion of each task.
Feasibility and Acceptability Measures

Parent and  Child Treatment Satisfaction (Ollendick et  al. 
2009)  Parents and children each completed a five-item 
measure of treatment satisfaction. Each item was rated on 
a five-point scale (e.g., 1 = not at all helpful, 5 = very, very 
helpful). Parents and children also rated fear, avoidance, and 
coping for phobic objects as compared to peers. Perceived 
interference, helpfulness of treatment, and willingness to 
recommend treatment to children with similar problems 
were also rated.
Procedures

Following Institutional Review Board (IRB) approval, par-
ticipants were recruited by contacting mental health profes-
sionals and pediatricians in the area, posting flyers at local 
restaurants and child friendly venues, emailing study infor-
mation to special education directors, utilizing an existing 
Autism Research Registry, and presenting information about 
the study at local parent trainings. Interested parents (N = 11) 
completed a brief phone screen with a research associate 
(about 15 min). Children who met eligibility criteria per the 
phone screen (N = 11) were scheduled for an initial assess-
ment. Families were offered $100 for completing the pre, 
post, and follow up assessments for a total of $300 for com-
pleting the study.
Informed consent and assent were obtained during the 
family’s first visit to the clinic. A project consensus meet-
ing, led by a licensed clinical psychologist, was held fol-
lowing the initial assessment to discuss findings from the 
Anxiety Disorders Interview Schedule for Children, Parent 
and Child Versions (Silverman and Albano 1996), establish 
initial diagnoses, and determine further eligibility for par-
ticipation. Clinicians utilized all available data during this 
consensus meeting including information from the ADIS-
5-P/C, the ADOS-2, and SSP, as well as parent records of 
prior community testing if such information was available. 
All children included in the study were determined during 
this meeting to meet DSM-5 criteria for ASD and to have 
SP. After completion of initial assessments, one family opted 
not to participate in the study and one family was referred 
out due to the presence of severe comorbid psychopathology 
resulting in a total of 9 participants in the treatment protocol.
After completing the initial eligibility assessment, partici-
pants were randomized to one of three baseline conditions

1020
	
Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders (2021) 51:1015–1027

(3, 4, or 5-week baseline periods). At each baseline ses-
sion, both the parent and child provided a fear rating for 
the child’s primary specific phobia (0 to 8 scale) and the 
child completed the BAT. In order to maintain independence 
of baseline and follow-up assessments, no participant was 
assigned to be treated by the clinicians who conducted the 
assessments.
The treatment consisted of one visit to the clinic to com-
plete the OST (for detailed description of the treatment 
see Ollendick and Ost (2001); Ollendick et al. 2009, 2015), 
followed by four 1-h booster sessions completed at the clinic 
during the 4 weeks immediately following treatment. In the 
unmodified, manual-based iteration of OST, booster ses-
sions are not provided; rather four 15-min phone calls with 
caregivers are provided to problem-solve at-home expo-
sure practices conducted by families following treatment. 
Given that children with ASD often demonstrate difficulty 
generalizing skills learned in treatment (White et al. 2018), 
OST procedures were modified to conduct longer in-person 
booster sessions to help maintain the gains obtained from 
OST and to facilitate generalization of the skills learned dur-
ing treatment.
The 3-h treatment session was divided into three 1-h 
blocks. Although the pacing of exposure activities was 
tailored to each participant individually, the 1st h typi-
cally consisted of psychoeducation (e.g., reading books or 
information sheets on phobic stimuli) and low-level expo-
sure activities (e.g., looking at pictures or videos of feared 
objects). The 2nd h of treatment often included initial in-
vivo exposures in a controlled manner (e.g., bug in a jar, 
dog on a leash). Primary caregivers joined treatment at this 
phase. Participants were encouraged to complete additional 
exposure steps at their own pace, with positive reinforcement 
consistently provided by both clinicians and caregivers. Pro-
gress was marked and rewarded using a visual aid (described 
below). The final hour of the treatment typically consisted of 
exposure to the phobic stimulus in a more naturalistic setting 
in order to provide opportunities for generalization (e.g.,

feeding treats to a dog off-leash, going outdoors to search 
for bugs, listening to sirens at a firehouse).
During the 3-h session, several modifications were made 
to make the treatment more acceptable to children with 
ASD. To provide a more concrete illustration of modifica-
tion of exposure activities, sample procedures for a limited 
number of participants are outlined in Table 3. For example, 
the parent was brought into the session during the 2nd h of 
treatment and remained in the room for the entire 3rd h. 
This is a departure from previous OST treatment protocols 
in which the parent is not in the room at all or is only in the 
room for part of the 3rd h of treatment. Additionally, dur-
ing the 3-h treatment a visual schedule was used to support 
children in tracking their progress. The schedule consisted 
of an image of a road with a set number of stops; children 
progressed along the road by completing various exposure 
steps, earning a break at the end of each hour by reaching 
the end of the road. Clinicians also asked children and their 
parents about any special interests their child might have 
and attempted to incorporate these interests into treatment. 
Families completed weekly practice logs with information 
about their continued exposures completed at home. At the 
end of the four booster sessions families retuned to the clinic 
for a post-treatment assessment session 1 week and 6 months 
following treatment.
Study Design and Data Analysis

The research design was a non-concurrent, multiple base-
line, single-case design. This design was selected as single-
case designs are often more cost effective and time effec-
tive than larger scale RCTs and hence, are often preferable 
for determining feasibility early in a treatment’s develop-
ment (Horner et al. 2005; Morgan 2009). Additionally, a 
non-concurrent design was selected in order to most closely 
re-create the conditions likely to be encountered in a com-
munity clinic in which children present in a sequential order 
at various times of year and in the midst of various commu-
nity events. Given the small sample size, the pre, post, and

follow-up data were analyzed using non-parametric Frei-
dman tests followed by post hoc Wilcoxon tests to examine 
changes from pre to post treatment, and post treatment to 
follow-up. Additionally, the Reliable Change Index (Jacob-
son and Truax 1991) was calculated to determine whether 
there were clinically significant reductions in ADIS scores.
Results
Treatment Adherence and Retention

All nine families completed treatment and the post-treat-
ment assessment 1 week after the last booster session. 
Six of the nine families completed the 6-month follow-up 
assessment. Two families did not respond to the clinic after 
three attempts were made to contact them for the 6-month 
follow-up and one family indicated it was impossible to get

1022
	
Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders (2021) 51:1015–1027

their child to leave the house due to (non-phobia related) 
increased psychopathology. Although these three partici-
pants were not responsive or not able to come in for the 
follow-up session that took place 6 months after treatment, 
these three families (Participants 3, 4, and 9) did complete 
the post-treatment assessment the week following treat-
ment and reported improvement in their child’s phobia 
symptoms at that time (See Fig. 1).
Changes in Phobia Severity (Table 4)

Friedman tests were conducted to examine changes in the 
CSRs assigned from the ADIS-P at pre, post, and follow-up 
assessments. Results of Friedman tests demonstrated 
significant and a large effect size reduction in symptoms 
on the ADIS-P overall (χ2(2) = 10.38, p = .006, W = 0.58). 
CSR means changed from 5.89 at pre-treatment, to 2.33 at 
post-treatment, and 2.17 at follow-up. Using a Bonferroni 
corrected p-value of 0.017, post-hoc Signed Rank Wilcoxon 
tests found significant reductions in phobia severity from pre 
to post (Z = − 2.69, p = .007), but not from post to follow-up 
(Z = − 0.54, p = .590).

Reliable Change Index (RCI) scores were also calculated 
for Specific Phobia CSR scores on the ADIS-P to meas-
ure clinically significant changes from pre to post, and pre 
to 6-month follow-up. Analyses showed that six (67%) of 
the nine children exceeded the clinical cutoff (> 1.96) for 
change in specific phobia scores from pre to post and that six 
(67%) of the nine children were in the non-clinical range at 
post-assessment (CSR < 4). For the pre to 6-month follow-
up, four (67%) of the six children who completed follow-up 
assessment exceeded the clinical cutoff (> 1.96) for change 
between their pre and 6-month CSR scores, and four (67%) 
of the six children who completed the 6-month follow-up 
fell in the non-clinical range at post-assessment (CSR < 4) 
as well.

Friedman tests were also conducted to examine changes 
in the CSRs assigned by clinicians on the ADIS-C at pre, 
post, and follow-up assessments. Results of Friedman tests 
demonstrated significant reduction in symptoms on the 
ADIS-C overall with a large effect size (χ2(2) = 9.29, p < .01, 
W = 0.53). CSR means changed from 5.67 at pre, to 2.50 at 
post-treatment, and 3.17 at follow-up. Using a Bonferroni 
corrected p-value of 0.017, post-hoc Signed Rank Wilcoxon 
tests found significant reductions in phobia severity from pre 
to post (Z = − 2.54, p = .011), but not from post-treatment to 
follow-up (Z = − 1.34, p = .180).
Reliable Change Index (RCI) scores were also calculated 
for Specific Phobia CSR scores on the ADIS-C to measure 
clinically significant changes from pre to post, and pre to 
6-month follow-up. Analyses showed that seven (78%) of the 
nine children exceeded the clinical cutoff (> 1.96) for change 
in specific phobia scores from pre to post and that seven 
(78%) of the nine children were in the non-clinical range at 
post-assessment (CSR < 4). For the pre to 6-month follow-
up, three (50%) of the six children who completed follow-up 
assessment exceeded the clinical cutoff (> 1.96) for change 
between their pre and 6-month CSR scores, with four (67%) 
of the six children who completed the 6-month follow-up in 
the non-clinical range at post-assessment (CSR < 4).
Changes in Avoidance Behavior (BAT; Table 4)

Children completed the BAT alone and with their parents 
at pre, post, and follow-up sessions. The percentage of BAT 
steps completed was calculated for each baseline session, 
as well as for the post and follow-up sessions. In order to 
statistically examine changes from pre-treatment to post and 
follow-up, per cent of steps completed during the first base-
line session was used. Signed Rank Wilcoxon tests were 
used to compare the percentage of BAT steps completed by 
the child alone vs. with parent at pre, post, and follow-up. At 
pre (Z = − 1.22, p = .223), post (Z = − 0.82, p = .414), and 
follow-up (Z = − 1.41, p = .157), there were no significant 
differences between the percentages of BAT steps completed

1023
Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders (2021) 51:1015–1027

alone vs. with a parent, although in general more steps were 
completed in the parent present condition. Friedman tests 
were conducted to examine changes in the percentage of 
steps completed alone at the pre (M = .60 [60%], SD = .37), 
post (M = .74 [74%], SD = .33), and follow-up (M = .85 
[85%], SD = .27) assessments. Although improvements were 
noted, results of Friedman tests demonstrated no significant 
reduction in avoidance between pre-treatment assessment to 
follow-up (χ2(2) = .353, p = .838). Because the overall analy-
sis was not significant, post-hoc tests were not conducted 
to examine changes from pre to post and post to follow-up. 
Still, it was evident that 5 of the 9 participants (55.55%) 
completed 70% or more of the steps at post-treatment, and 
at follow-up.
Friedman tests were also conducted to examine changes 
in the percentage of steps completed with a parent at the pre 
(M = .80 [80%], SD = .26), post (M = .83 [83%], SD = .25), 
and follow-up (M = .88 [88%], SD = .24) assessments. 
Results of Friedman tests demonstrated no significant 
reduction in avoidance with a parent present between pre-
treatment assessment to follow-up (χ2(2) = 2.00, p = .368). 
Because the overall analysis was not significant, post-hoc 
tests were not conducted to examine changes from pre to 
post and post to follow-up. With parent present, seven of 
the nine participants (77.77%) completed 70% or more of 
the steps at post-treatment and five of the six participants 
(83.33%) that remained at follow-up were able to complete 
70% or more of steps at follow-up.

Changes in Parent Report of Fear (Parent Fear 
Ratings; Fig. 1; Table 4)

Friedman tests were conducted to examine changes in the 
parent ratings of their child’s fear on a scale of 0–8 at pre, 
post, and follow-up assessments. Parent fear ratings across 
baseline sessions were averaged to create a pre-treatment 
parent fear rating for the purpose of statistical analyses. 
Results of Friedman tests demonstrated significant reduc-
tion in parent fear ratings overall (χ2(2) = 7.90, p = .019). 
Parent fear rating means changed from 6.98 at pre, to 3.22 
at post-treatment, and 3.33 at follow-up. Post-hoc Signed 
Rank Wilcoxon tests found significant reductions in parent 
fear ratings from pre to post (Z = − 2.67, p = .008), but not 
from post to follow-up (Z = − .14, p = .891).
Satisfaction Results

Parents generally reported a high level of satisfaction with 
their child’s improvement following treatment. At 1-week 
post-treatment, on average, parents reported that their child 
was only “a little bit fearful” (M = 2.33, SD = 1), and “a little 
bit avoidant” (M = 2.33, SD = .707) compared to same-age 
peers. Five of nine families (55%) also indicated they would

agree with the statement that their child is now better able to 
cope with his fear or phobia. Two families (22%) indicated 
that they strongly agreed with this statement, and one family 
(11%) indicated that they felt neutral about this statement. 
Overall, families indicated that after treatment their child’s 
fear interferes with their life only “a little bit” (M = 1.78, 
SD = .667). Seven of the nine families (78%) indicated that 
they strongly agreed with the statement that treatment was 
helpful for their child, while two families (22%) indicated 
that they agreed with this statement. Finally, eight of the 
nine families (88%) indicated that they strongly agreed with 
the statement that they would recommend a treatment like 
this to a family with a similar problem (the other family 
indicated that they agreed with this statement).
Discussion

In this study, we evaluated the feasibility and acceptabil-
ity, as well as the initial efficacy, of a modified brief and 
intensive treatment for nine children with a SP who also 
had a diagnosis of ASD. Consistent with recent trends in 
psychotherapy research, we sought to determine if a brief 
and intensive treatment would be effective for youth with 
ASD, as they have been shown to be with typically devel-
oping youth (Davis et al. 2019). In general, these brief and 
intensive treatment approaches have modified more tradi-
tional or conventional CBT approaches by reducing either 
the number of sessions or the time period over which the 
sessions are delivered (see Öst and Ollendick 2017). Here, 
the initial treatment was delivered in one 3-h session as is 
done with typically developing children. However, we made 
several modifications in the delivery of the treatment and 
we followed the 1-session treatment with four 1-h booster 
sessions delivered for 4 weeks immediately following treat-
ment. During the one 3-h session and the four 1-h booster 
sessions, modifications were made to make the treatment 
more accessible to, and potentially effective for, children 
with ASD. More specifically, the parent was brought into 
the session during the 2nd h of treatment and remained in 
the room for the entire second and 3rd h, as well as during 
each of the four booster sessions. Additionally, during the 
3-h treatment session and the booster sessions, a visual token 
board was used so that children could visually track their 
progress each hour. We also determined any special inter-
ests the child might have and attempted to incorporate those 
interests into treatment. We added these features following 
the recommendations of Davis et al. (2007,2019) and our 
clinical experience in working with youth who have ASD 
and a SP. More specifically, we perceived the need to sup-
plement the one 3-h session with additional booster sessions 
to secure greater maintenance effects following treatment. 
We hypothesized that these modifications would not only be

1024
	
Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders (2021) 51:1015–1027

feasible but that they would also result in high levels of treat-
ment satisfaction, that the children would exhibit significant 
changes in the severity of their phobias and their behavioral 
avoidance of the feared stimuli following treatment, and that 
parental fear ratings of their children would also decrease.
Overall, our study provides initial support for the efficacy 
of this brief and intensive treatment for SPs in youth who 
have ASD. Results demonstrated improvements in parent 
report of their children’s fears as well as reductions in the 
clinical severity of the children’s phobias as determined by 
independent clinicians. Although gains were limited for 
some children (see Table 4 and Fig. 1), significant reductions 
in clinical severity on the ADIS-P and ADIS-C were evident. 
On the parent interview, CSR means changed from 5.89 at 
pre-treatment to 2.33 at post-treatment and 2.17 at follow-up, 
both of the latter ratings were below the diagnostic thresh-
old of < 4.0 and, importantly, the gains at post-treatment 
were maintained at 6-month follow-up. Of special note, 6 
of the 9 children (67%) were below this clinical threshold 
at the post-treatment assessment; moreover, reliable change 
indices were also observed for 67% at both post-treatment 
and 6-month follow-up. Thus, significant clinical changes 
were noted. Similar gains were noted on the ADIS-C with 
reductions in CSRs from 5.67 to 2.50 at post-treatment. At 
6-month follow-up, a slight increase to 3.17 was noted; how-
ever, both of the latter ratings were below the diagnostic 
threshold of < 4.0. Importantly, the gains at post-treatment 
were maintained at 6-month follow-up.
As noted above, all nine families completed treatment 
and the 1-week post-treatment assessment. Although three 
of the nine families did not complete the 6-month follow-
up assessment, there were no differences observed between 
the families who did and who did not return for the follow-
up assessment. All nine children showed similar changes at 
post-treatment and children who completed follow-up did 
not differ from children who did not complete follow-up on 
pre-treatment assessment variables (See Table 1, Partici-
pants 3, 4, and 9). Furthermore, parents generally reported 
a high level of satisfaction with treatment, that their children 
were now better able to cope with their previously feared 
phobic stimuli, and that their children’s fear only interfered 
minimally with their and their families’ lives following treat-
ment. They also indicated that treatment was not only help-
ful for their children but that they would recommend this 
treatment to a family with similar problems. Thus, the treat-
ment was not only feasible to implement but it also resulted 
in high levels of treatment satisfaction. Interestingly, none 
of the parents provided any negative feedback regarding the 
treatment.
These positive findings were shown in our non-concur-
rent multiple baseline design. Importantly, we were able to 
show relatively stable baseline measures of parental fear rat-
ings across the 3, 4, and 5-week baseline sessions prior to

treatment, allowing for greater confidence in establishing 
a relationship between our treatment and behavior change 
(Kazdin 1998; Watson and Workman 1981). Moreover, as 
Hawkins et al. (2007) note, in order for a multiple base-
line study to demonstrate methodological rigor, it must (1) 
show a change in the targeted behavior (i.e., fear levels and 
behavioral avoidance), (2) demonstrate that the change is 
likely due to the intervention, and (3) indicate that clinically 
significant and practical changes were evident. The current 
study clearly meets two of these criteria by showing clini-
cally significant changes in both the clinician-determined 
clinical severity of the phobia and parent ratings of fear 
levels for their children following intervention. Although 
significant reductions in behavioral avoidance on the BAT 
were not observed, positive trends were nonetheless present. 
More specifically, following treatment, 55.55% (5 of 9) of 
the youth were able to complete over 70% of the steps in 
the alone condition and 77.77% (7 of 9) were able to com-
plete over 70% of the steps in the parent condition. These 
percentages indicate considerable reductions, albeit non-
significantly, in behavioral avoidance. Quite obviously, it 
will be important to explore the efficacy of this treatment in 
a randomized control trial as well as determine the children 
and families for whom this treatment will be most effec-
tive. It will also be important to determine whether all of 
the modifications made in this study are necessary through 
a dismantling approach while also exploring mediators of 
change. Most important will be to explore whether the four 
1-h booster sessions are critical or if our standard 10–15 min 
check in would be sufficient as it has been shown to be with 
typically developing youth and their families. Still, despite 
the need for continued research, the brief intervention as 
delivered in this study appears promising.
Finally, it may be important to note the discrepancy 
between the presentation of the phobias in this study and 
Kerns and Kendall’s (2012) suggestion that children with 
ASD tend to experience more “atypical” fears. In this sam-
ple, one child presented with what might be considered an 
“atypical” fear of chewing gum; however, the other phobias 
were comparable to what might be seen in typically develop-
ing children (e.g., insects, the dark, storms, and heights). A 
larger study is needed to determine if this finding is a result 
of a small sample size or if, in fact, children with ASD expe-
rience fears not so discrepant from those of their typically 
developing peers.
From a clinical perspective, the children and their par-
ents’ responses to our brief and intensive treatment sug-
gest that the treatment might be an effective and practical 
alternative to more extended and weekly interventions for 
such problems. Compared to more traditional treatments, 
this approach allows the participants more flexibility in the 
timing and the delivery of the treatment. In addition, this 
modified treatment seems more suitable to families from

1025
Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders (2021) 51:1015–1027

rural areas and for families in which barriers to treatment 
such as transportation, childcare, or finances may be present. 
This treatment does require greater parental participation, 
which some studies suggest results in greater improvement 
in child anxiety for children with ASD than child-alone treat-
ments (Davis et al. 2007; Wood et al. 2006). In addition, 
this relatively brief treatment might be used as a first line 
treatment to target specific phobias before recommending 
more extensive clinic-based treatments. Using a stepped care 
approach, this intervention can be “stepped up” if signifi-
cant changes are not documented (Ollendick et al. 2018a, b). 
Given the stability of effects from post-treatment to follow-
up, improvements can be monitored over a period of time 
before deciding to seek further treatments.
These generally positive findings must be viewed within 
certain study limitations. First off, as with all single case 
design studies, we are unable to rule out the potential for 
response bias in the reporting of our parents or the children. 
Parents and children knew they were receiving a treatment 
that had been shown to be effective for typically developing 
children and it is possible that some degree of social desir-
ability in reporting to us may have been present. This pos-
sibility might also account for the fact that no families pro-
vided us negative feedback regarding the treatment and its 
implementation. Second, it is also possible that our assessors 
may have been biased in their ratings of improvement fol-
lowing treatment. We were careful however to review their 
ratings in our clinical consensus meetings. Moreover, none 
of the assessors completed treatment on the youth and fami-
lies they assessed. Still, they were not blind to the purposes 
of the study. Finally, there may be limitations associated 
with our clinical sample of ASD youth and their respec-
tive phobias. All of the youth were boys, were verbal, and 
possessed IQs above 80; moreover, the majority were from 
middle or upper-middle class families, and, for the most 
part, had specific phobias that were not unlike those of typi-
cally developing youth. Accordingly, it will be important to 
examine the feasibility, acceptability, and efficacy of this 
adapted procedure with youth and families differing in these 
characteristics.
In sum, within the limitations noted, this study illustrates 
the potential efficacy of this modified one-session treatment 
for youth with SP and comorbid ASD. Once again, however, 
randomized controlled trials are needed to test the relative 
effectiveness of this modified approach to the more standard 
approach and to determine the relative effectiveness of both 
approaches with youth who have ASD. Nevertheless, this 
pilot study provides preliminary evidence for the feasibility, 
acceptability and effectiveness of this brief and intensive 
treatment with youth who have ASD.
